retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Biopsies -- Technique or Treatment -- Marsupialization and Drain Placement
retrieved_docs.content: Drains are an excellent procedural adjunct that facilitates decompression in bony cystic lesions and other entities like the odontogenic keratocyst. After representative tissue is taken during the biopsy, a drain can be placed into the lesion and sutured to the outer mucosa or wired to nearby teeth. When combined with daily postoperative irrigation, a drain can shrink large lesions dramatically, facilitating enucleation and curettage for a lesion that would otherwise have required extensive resection. Excellent materials for drains include pediatric nasopharyngeal airways or various intravenous tubing trimmed to the appropriate size. Drains may be left in place if the lesion continues to shrink objectively on radiography and the patient can tolerate the foreign body. Drains should be placed to not interfere with occlusion and minimize damage to surrounding tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Corneal Foreign Body -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Epithelial inclusion cysts: Localized corneal epithelial cysts that resemble retained material.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Differential Diagnosis -- Residual Cyst
retrieved_docs.content: Unicystic ameloblastoma odontogenic keratocyst Glandular odontogenic keratocyst Lateral periodontal cyst

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Biopsies -- Technique or Treatment -- Bony Lesion
retrieved_docs.content: After administration of local anesthesia, an incision is made to expose the bony area of the lesion. If the bone is relatively thin, needle aspiration of the lesion should be performed. If the lesion is vascular, this pinpoint bleeding can be controlled, hemostasis achieved, and the site closed. Angiography, other diagnostic studies, or consultation for possible preoperative embolization can be scheduled to prevent potentially life-threatening bleeding. [53] The lesion is likely cystic if aspiration yields a clear, turbid, yellow fluid. If aspiration yields a caseous substance, the lesion is highly suspicious for an odontogenic keratocyst. If nothing is aspirated, this indicates a more solid tumor or tissue. An incisional biopsy is preferred for solid lesions; the differential diagnosis for solid lesions includes more entities that require margins of up to 1 cm and further treatment planning before definitive treatment. If the bone is too thick to enter with a needle directly, a burr can be used to thin the bone until a needle can be introduced. After aspiration, the lesion is entered with a burr under irrigation.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjunctival Concretions -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Epidermal inclusion cyst: can be congenital or acquired. These are cystic lesions in the subepithelium space but have clear fluid, unlike concretions that are solid and filled with keratin. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Branchial Cleft Cysts -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Branchial cleft cysts are characterized by an epithelial lining composed of stratified squamous epithelium, which may contain keratinous debris within the cyst. In certain instances, the cyst wall may be lined by ciliated columnar epithelium, resulting in a more mucoid consistency of the contents. Lymphoid tissue typically surrounds the epithelial lining. Infection or rupture can introduce inflammatory cells into the cavity or stroma (see Image . Branchial Cyst on Histopathology).

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Differential Diagnosis -- Odontogenic Keratocyst
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastoma Dentigerous cyst Orthokeratinizing odontogenic cyst

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Cysts -- Anatomical Pathology -- Epidermal inclusion cysts (ie, epidermoid cysts, infundibular cysts)
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic features of epidermal inclusion cysts include: Open unilocular cyst (rarely multilocular) Stratified squamous epithelium with epidermal keratinization (ie, keratohyaline granules and flat surface epithelium) with retained granular layer (see Image. Epidermoid Cyst) Lamellated or shredded-appearing keratin without hair shafts (see Image. Epidermoid Cyst Lining) Keratin fragments (if ruptured), which may be the only clue Focal inflammation with features of abscess formation, granulation tissue, foreign body granulomatous reaction, or dermal fibrosis Multiple variants of epidermal inclusion cysts exist in the literature, which include:

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Cysts -- Anatomical Pathology -- Steatocystoma
retrieved_docs.content: Lesions without associated sebaceous glands are termed cutaneous keratocysts. They may be seen in association with basal cell nevus syndrome, usually as epidermal cysts with thick brown fluid rather than keratocysts of the jaw. Cutaneous keratocysts have a festooned configuration with squamous epithelium and no granular layer. Expression of D2-40 in the basal layer of cutaneous keratocysts has been described in basal cell nevus syndrome–associated cutaneous keratocysts. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Branchial Cleft Cysts -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle aspiration cytology may be less reliable than frozen sections following surgical excision of cystic masses. The false negative rate for fine needle aspiration cytology in diagnosing squamous cell carcinoma from cystic metastases exceeds 50%, mainly due to insufficient diagnostic cellular material. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Downgrowth -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: More aggressive surgical procedures for epithelial downgrowth vary greatly in technique and success rates and depend on the location and structures affected. In some situations, epithelial cysts can be treated more conservatively, which may be recommended in children to preserve intraocular structures and manage amblyopia. One such technique consists of the viscodissection of the cyst with the aspiration of cyst contents and photocoagulation. [34] These procedures still run the risk of recurrence or converting a cyst into the diffuse form. Therefore, complete excision of cysts along with affected intraocular structures with full-thickness corneoscleral grafting may provide the most definitive surgical management. [17] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Cysts -- Anatomical Pathology -- Branchial cleft cysts
retrieved_docs.content: The origin of branchial cleft cysts is not precisely known, but they may be from branchial cleft mucosal remnants and cystic changes in cervical chain lymph nodes; it is thought that material from the second branchial pouch leads to the formation of cysts on the anterior sternocleidomastoid in young patients, while material from the first branchial pouch leads to the formation of cysts at the auricular or mandibular sites. [53] These cysts present laterally located cystic nodules in the preauricular area, along the mandible, or anywhere from the jaw's angle to the sternocleidomastoid muscle's anterior border. [54] Lesions are typically evident in childhood or early adulthood. [55] The microscopic features of branchial cleft cysts include a stratified squamous or pseudostratified columnar epithelium, which may be ciliated, striking lymphoid follicle formation, and turbid fluid rich in cholesterol (see Image. Branchial Cyst).

retrieved_docs.title: Branchial Cleft Anomalies -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Branchial cleft cysts are lined with stratified squamous epithelium and may contain keratinous debris inside the cyst. In some cases, the cyst wall is lined by ciliated columnar epithelium, resulting in more mucoid contents. Lymphoid tissue is typically present surrounding the epithelial lining. If the cyst is infected or ruptured, inflammatory cells can be identified in the cavity or stroma (see Image. Branchial Cyst on Histopathology). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Keratoacanthoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Considered by some to be a highly differentiated form of squamous cell carcinoma, histological examination reveals a circumscribed proliferation of well-differentiated keratinocytes (see Image. Keratoacanthoma). This has been described as multilobular exophytic or endophytic cyst-like invagination of the epidermis. The epidermis extends over the tumor, and there is a central horn plug of keratin. Peripheral to the keratin-filled crater are lip-like, peripheral borders of the epidermis. Intraepidermal neutrophilic abscesses are visualized in addition to horn pearls. The cells of the keratoacanthoma tumor are enlarged and atypical keratinocytes. They have a cytoplasm described as eosinophilic. Due to 3 stages of solitary keratoacanthoma, the histological examination can vary between stages. In comparison to squamous cell carcinoma, keratoacanthoma has intraepidermal microabscesses and tissue eosinophilia more commonly found. Recently keratoacanthoma has been reclassified as squamous cell carcinoma keratoacanthoma type (SCC-KA). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Evaluation -- Odontogenic Keratocyst
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 25 to 40% of odontogenic keratocysts are associated with an impacted tooth.[10]. Clinically, the patient may have delayed tooth eruption, and thus, it is important to obtain diagnostic radiographs to evaluate. OKCs are frequently asymptomatic as they expand in an anterior-posterior direction with little buccolingual expansion. Larger OKCs may present with pain, intraoral edema, trismus, sensory deficits, infection, and drainage. Radiographically, OKCs have varied presentations ranging from a well-demarcated unilocular lesion with smooth borders to a unilocular lesion with a scalloped border to a multilocular radiolucency. [2] Patients presenting with multiple OKC lesions should be evaluated for or referred to evaluate for nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome (Gorlin syndrome). Gorlin syndrome is an autosomal dominant inherited condition resulting from mutations in the PTCH tumor suppressor gene mapped to chromosome 9q22. The syndrome is significant for multiple basal cell carcinomas, palmar pits, multiple OKCs, and bilamellar calcification of the falx cerebri. Genetic testing of the patient and close family members is required to diagnose Gorlin syndrome officially. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Corneal Foreign Body -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Fungal or acanthamoeba keratitis: Organic material or infiltrates that may be misdiagnosed as foreign bodies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2143
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi constitute a procedural category that includes several distinct therapeutic and diagnostic interventions. Within this category, Endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea is performed to extract objects lodged in the airway. Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed with therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (initial) is used to clear secretions or debris and to assess airway pathology at the start of a hospital admission. A subsequent Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, with therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (subsequent, same hospital stay) provides follow‑up clearance or reassessment during the same admission. Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus involves bronchoscopic removal of abnormal tissue such as tumors or polyps. Recognizing that these four procedures are child procedures of the broader Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi helps clinicians organize airway management strategies, select the appropriate technique for foreign body removal, aspiration, or lesion excision, and coordinate sequential care when multiple bronchoscopic interventions are required during a single hospital stay.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: trauma, surgery, age‑related degeneration, calcific deposition, congenital malformations, and metastatic cancer each generate distinct but often overlapping structural and functional disturbances in the posterior segment, necessitating comprehensive ocular assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1113
retrieved_docs.content: procedural instances, clarifies the anatomical focus on the tracheobronchial structure and lung, delineates methods used for tumor destruction, abscess drainage, and foreign‑body removal, and integrates the classification of bronchial‑lung malignancies, providing a comprehensive framework for bronchoscopic interventions in pulmonary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 575
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy all share the removal technique as their procedural method. The removal technique is further defined by the method_of relationship, where nonoperative removal of foreign body is recognized as a specific removal technique. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how various bronchoscopy and endoscopic procedures on the trachea and bronchi are organized under therapeutic categories, how they are sub‑classified, and which operative or non‑operative methods are employed to achieve clinical objectives such as tumor destruction, aspiration, lesion excision, and foreign‑body removal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 255
retrieved_docs.content: and is associated with the lung, indicating that aspirated material can lodge within pulmonary tissue. Surgical procedures in the lung (العمليات الجراحية في الرئة) are organized under the broader bronchoscopy hierarchy (Arabic: تنظيم القصبات), demonstrating that bronchoscopic interventions constitute a foundational platform for both diagnostic sampling and a wide range of therapeutic applications targeting the trachea, bronchi, and lung.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
retrieved_docs.content: Destruction of localized lesion of retina (eg, macular edema, tumors), one or more sessions; radiation by implantation of source is a specific destructive procedure on the posterior segment of the eye and is classified as a child (subtype) of posterior‑segment destructive procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 107
retrieved_docs.content: terminology aligns these concepts: تنظيم القصبات denotes bronchial or airway management procedures, while عمليات جراحية في الرئة (lung surgeries) are placed under this parent category. Overall, the knowledge base integrates diagnostic and therapeutic bronchoscopy, airway foreign‑body extraction, inflammatory and malignant lung diseases, and their anatomical sites, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing tracheobronchial and pulmonary conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 146
retrieved_docs.content: of tumor by laser surgery (a destructive surgical method), Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, Endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea, Rigid or flexible bronchoscopy with therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree, Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus, and Removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy. All foreign‑body removal interventions share a “removal technique” classification. The “bronchy” relationship connects these endoscopic procedures to the broader bronchoscopy domain, supporting accurate coding and clinical decision‑making. Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus form a subset of broader diagnostic categories and are directly associated with the lung. Bronchoscopy (“bronchy”) is a child concept of the lung and functions in both diagnostic and therapeutic capacities. Therapeutic interventions that employ the removal technique include endoscopic removal of an intraluminal foreign body from the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi constitute a group of airway endoscopic interventions that include several distinct therapeutic techniques. One of these techniques is Endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea, which involves using an endoscope to extract objects lodged within the tracheal lumen. Another procedure within this group is Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, subsequent, same hospital stay, a comprehensive approach that combines visual inspection, imaging assistance, and aspiration to clear secretions or debris from the tracheobronchial tree during a single admission. The category also encompasses Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus, a targeted endoscopic resection aimed at removing pathological lesions located in the bronchial airways. Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities offered by endoscopic techniques on the trachea and bronchi, highlighting their importance in managing airway foreign bodies, performing guided bronchoscopic interventions, and excising bronchial lesions within a unified clinical framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: tracheostomy with biopsy of lung, bronchoscopy with laser‑induced tumor destruction, bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess, bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea, two‑stage therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (rigid or flexible bronchoscopy with fluoroscopic guidance followed by a repeat procedure during the same admission), endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus, and removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy. All these procedures are linked by the “bronchy” classification, reflecting their placement within bronchoscopic endoscopic interventions. The removal technique is the shared procedural method for extracting intraluminal foreign bodies from the trachea, bronchus, or other airway segments, emphasizing bronchoscopy‑based approaches as the primary means for non‑operative foreign‑body extraction. Diagnostic procedures on lung and bronchus are organized under the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 74
retrieved_docs.content: laser‑induced tumor destruction * Bronchoscopy with drainage of lung abscess * Therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (rigid or flexible, fluoroscopic guidance) * Endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign bodies from the trachea or bronchus * Endoscopic excision of bronchial lesions Each procedure is linked to its direct procedural site (trachea, bronchi, lung) and to a method category (removal technique, destructive surgery, drainage, aspiration). Additional respiratory‑tract procedures are encoded as self‑classifying classes, such as respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation, tracheal intubation (تنبيب الرغامى), temporary or permanent tracheostomy, and placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Therapeutic and non‑operative airway procedures are grouped under “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system.” Surgical and radiotherapeutic concepts extend beyond the airway: thoracoscopic wedge resection of the lung,
